Facts.text: Ravenna becomes the seat of the Byzantine military governor in Italy

Taira no Munemori and the Taira clan take young Emperor Antoku and the three sacred treasures and flee to western Japan to escape the Minamoto clan (Traditional Japanese date: 25th Day of the 7th Month of the 2nd Year of Juei)
Portuguese defeat Castilians at Aljubarrota and retain independence
Bishop Frederik of Blankenheim of Utrecht occupies Coevorden
Battle of Domažlice: German imperial army takes flight and flees after hearing the Hussites sing their battle chorale, part of the Hussite Crusades
Ottoman fleet under Dragut captures Tripoli from the Knights of Malta after a six-day bombardment
Spanish explorer de Luna lands in Pensacola Bay, Florida
Publication of Christopher Marlowe's play "Tamburlaine the Great" is recorded by the Stationers' Company of London (likely first performed in 1587)
Dutch Nassau fleet abandons its blockade of the Spanish-held port of Callao near Lima in Peru after three months
Spanish troops occupy Corbie near Amiens
Abel Tasman's ships, Heemskerck and Zeehaen, depart from Batavia to search for Terra Australis, becoming the first Europeans to reach Tasmania and New Zealand
Battle of Mons: French repel William of Orange
Prussian army occupies Saxony, beginning the Second Silesian War
The French capture Fort Oswego, New York
Battle of Zorndorf: Prussia defeats Russia, with thousands killed
The English fleet occupies Havana
Massachusetts colonists challenge British rule by an elm tree (Liberty Tree)
Suriname forbids selling enslaved mothers without their babies
Sweden and Russia sign the Treaty of Väräla
The British warship Pelican attacks and captures US war brigantine Argus
Great Britain annexes Tristan da Cunha
The first US eye hospital, the NY Eye Infirmary, opens in New York City
General Lafayette returns to the US
Dutch King William I expels foreign students

US Army Colonel Worth declares the Second Seminole War over after nearly seven years; over 3,000 surviving members of the Seminole Nation are forcibly relocated from Florida to Oklahoma, with only about 300 permitted to remain
The Cape Girardeau meteorite, a 2.3 kg chondrite-type meteorite, strikes near the town of Cape Girardeau in Missouri
Oregon Territory is created
79th NY troops mutiny
Martial Law is declared in St. Louis, Missouri, due to pro-secession sentiment surging throughout Missouri after the Battle of Wilson's Creek
Confederate General Joe Wheeler besieges Dalton, Georgia, but withdraws on August 15, 1864
Second day of the Second Battle of Deep Bottom, Virginia: Federal assault
Field & Stream begins publishing
Society of Real Afrikaners establishes in Paarl
Prairie View State University is formed
British Criminal Law Amendment Act raises the age of consent from 13 to 16 and protects against child prostitution
Japan's first patent is issued to Zuisho Hotta for anticorrosive paint used on ship hulls
France introduces motor vehicle registration, including a driving test
Sir Oliver Joseph Lodge demonstrates wireless telegraphy (radio) using Morse code at a meeting of the British Association for the Advancement of Science at Oxford University
Anosimena is captured by French troops from Menabe defenders in Madagascar
First electric tram in Netherlands (Leidseplein-Brouwersgracht)
First claimed powered flight by Gustave Whitehead in his No. 21 aircraft near Bridgeport, Connecticut
SS Islander hits an iceberg near Alaska and sinks, killing 70
Ngindo-rebellion killed 5 RC German clergymen in East-Africa

Race riot in Springfield, Illinois; a white mob attacks a Black neighborhood when lynching plans are thwarted by local police, resulting in at least 17 people dying over two days; the event is a catalyst for the formation of the civil rights organization, the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP)
The first beauty contest is held in Folkestone, England
Indianapolis Motor Speedway in Speedway, Indiana, home of the Indy 500, hosts its first motorized event, a series of motorcycle races
6th International Congress of Esperantists held in Washington, D.C.
General Leconte appointed temporary President of Haiti
United States Senate leaders rotate the office of President pro tempore of the Senate among leading candidates to fill the vacancy left by William P. Frye's death
2,500 US Marines invade Nicaragua; the US remains until 1925
British transport Royal Edward is sunk by a German U-boat, killing 1,000
China declares war on Germany and Austria
Chicago White Sox outfielder Happy Felsch ties the MLB record with four outfield assists in a game during a 15-6 loss to the Boston Red Sox
Little Entente is formed by Czechoslovakia, Yugoslavia, and Romania
VII Summer Olympic Games open in Antwerp, Belgium; first time Olympic Oath is voiced, doves are released to symbolize peace, and Olympic Flag is flown
Tuvan People's Republic declares independence with the support of Soviet Russia
First "old-time" musicians' radio broadcast (Jenkins-WSB Atlanta)
Moccasin Powerhouse along the Hetch Hetchy Aqueduct in California is completed
Mount Rushmore Monument is first proposed
Ben Hecht and Charles MacArthur's "The Front Page" premieres in New York City
Jewish Agency for Palestine is formed
Brooklyn Dodgers reliever Johnny Quinn, 49, becomes the oldest pitcher to win an MLB game in a 2-1, 10th-inning victory over the NY Giants at the Polo Grounds
Philips makes its 1 millionth radio
X Summer Olympic Games close at the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum
Social Security Act becomes law
American swimmers Adolph Kiefer and Al Vande Weghe quinella the 100 m backstroke at the Berlin Olympics
Rainey Bethea is hanged in Owensboro, Kentucky in the last public execution in the United States
The Berlin Olympics rowing competition concludes with Germany winning 5 of 7 gold medals, although the United States wins the coveted eights gold
China declares war on Japan
Detroit Tigers score an MLB record 36 runs in a doubleheader vs. St. Louis Browns at Navin Field; win 16-1 and 20-7
The Appalachian Trail is formally completed, traversing 2,000 miles and 14 US states from Georgia to Maine
BBC's first feature film on TV (The Student of Prague)
First night MLB game in the city of Chicago is played at Comiskey Park; White Sox beat St. Louis Browns 5-2
First allied air raid on Borneo
Quadrant Conference over strike in Pacific Ocean is held in Quebec, Canada, August 14-24
US 45th Division occupies Falcone, 40 km outside of Messina
Canada and Poland begin Operation Tractable, attempting to encircle the strategic French town of Falaise
Operation Tractable: New Canadian offensive
Soviet offensive at Vistula
Pakistan gains independence from the United Kingdom
XIV Olympic Games close at Wembley Stadium, London
Military coup in Syria under colonel Sami Hinnawi
Mátyás Rákosi is appointed premier of Hungary
20th NFL Chicago All-Star Game: Detroit 24, All-Stars 10 (93,818 attendees)
KXLF TV channel 4 in Butte, MT (CBS/ABC) begins broadcasting
WGR TV (now WGRZ) TV channel 2 in Buffalo, NY (NBC) begins broadcasting
Canadian Football League plays its first game (Winnipeg 29, Edmonton 21)
Cleveland Indian Vic Power steals home twice in one game
KLM Super Constellation crashes west of Ireland, killing 99
26th NFL Chicago All-Star Game: Baltimore 29, All-Stars 0 (70,000 attendees)

The first American Football League meeting is held in Chicago; charter memberships are handed to Dallas, New York, Houston, Denver, Los Angeles, and Minneapolis-Saint Paul; the AFL is named the following week
Australian driver Jack Brabham clinches his second consecutive F1 World Drivers' Championship by winning the Portuguese Grand Prix at Boavista
UN peacekeeping troops are deployed to the Republic of Congo
Philadelphia Phillies lose 9-2 to Chicago Cubs for their 17th straight defeat; 11th consecutive time an opposing pitcher throws a complete game against the Phillies
A US mail truck in Plymouth, Massachusetts, is robbed of more than $1.5 million
French and Italian workers break through at Mont Blanc Vehicular Tunnel
Egypt, Iraq, Jordan, Kuwait, and Syria form a common market
LA Angels pitcher Bo Belinsky is suspended after a hotel room fight with elderly Los Angeles Times sportswriter Braven Dyer
Continental Football League plays first games
Cleveland Stadium's first rock concert: The Beatles headline, and fans break through barriers and rush the stage, causing a 30-minute delay while order is restored
First US lunar orbiter begins orbiting Moon
Belgian embassy in Kinshasa, Congo, plundered
British pirate radio station Radio 270, broadcast from an offshore lugger, is forced to close down
Pirate radio station, Wonderful Radio London, closes down
Radio Scotland and Radio Swinging Holland go off the air
Montreal Expos officially become a member of NL
British Army deploys on the streets of Northern Ireland, beginning Operation Banner
Irish nationalists hold protests throughout Northern Ireland in response to events in Derry, and some of these become violent
NY Mets fall 9½ games back and later win pennant
City University of New York inaugurates open admissions
Steven Stills is arrested for drug possession
Bahrain proclaims independence after 110 years of British rule
British begin internment without trial in Northern Ireland
Cardinals' Bob Gibson, 35, no-hits Pirates, 11-0
France performs a nuclear test
A Catholic civilian is shot dead during an IRA attack on a British Army patrol in Belfast, Northern Ireland
Two British soldiers are killed by an IRA booby trap bomb in Belfast, Northern Ireland
US ends secret bombing of Cambodia
Congress authorizes US citizens to own gold
Turkish army attacks Nicosia Cyprus
US performs a nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
USSR performs an underground nuclear test
King Olav V of Norway opens Svalbard Airport near Longyearbyen, the northernmost airport in the world with scheduled public flights
10,000 Northern Ireland women demonstrate for peace in Belfast
Stiff Records releases its first single, "So It Goes," by rocker Nick Lowe
77,691 people see NY Cosmos beat Fort Lauderdale Strikers 8-3 at Giants Stadium
A rainbow is seen in the north of Wales for a three-hour duration
17,000 workers strike at the Lenin Shipyard in Gdańsk, Poland, beginning the Solidarity movement
George Foster hits his 8th home run into the red seats at Riverfront
Phillies'Mike Schmidthits his 300th career home run off New York Met Mike Scott
Atlanta snaps an 11-game losing streak with a 6-5 win over the Padres
Iran "Ramadan offensive" in Iraq
Ramones guitarist Johnny Ramone suffers a fractured skull from a kick to the head in a fight over a woman in Queens, NYC, and undergoes brain surgery
Robert de Castella wins Helsinki marathon (2:10:03)
IBM releases PC DOS version 3.0
West Indies complete 5-0 series annihilation of England
Political violence by the youth begins after the funeral of assassinated Victoria Mxenge, a civil rights lawyer respected and liked by the Congress of South African Students
Phillies and Pirates play a six-game series in four days
Detroit beats Sox 18-6 at Fenway, ending Boston's winning streak at 24
Angel's Luis Polonia is 74th to hit an inside-the-park grand slam
Denver votes for a 1% sales tax to pay for a baseball franchise
Nigerian presidential election
St. Louis reliever Lee Smith is the fastest to achieve 40 saves
MLB pitcher Doc Gooden completes substance abuse rehabilitation at the Betty Ford Center in Rancho Mirage, California
Space telescope Hubble photographs Uranus with its rings
Terrorist Carlos the Jackal is captured in Khartoum, Sudan
Iran's official news agency, IRNA, reports that Iran has been unable to sell 200 million barrels of crude oil per day since the imposition of a unilateral oil embargo by the US
Shannon Faulkner becomes the first woman to attend The Citadel
Soyuz TM-25 lands

New Zealand wins their third Tri Nations Rugby Series as South Africa edges Australia 10-9 in Cape Town; the All Blacks' only defeat comes in the final round dead rubber, 28-7 against the Wallabies in Sydney
Widespread power blackout in the northeastern United States and Canada
The Australian women's 4 x 100 m freestyle relay team of Alice Mills, Lisbeth Lenton, Petria Thomas, and Jodie Henry smashes the world record to take gold in 3:35.94 at the Athens Olympics
Coordinated bombings in Yazidi communities in Iraq kill at least 500 people, the second-deadliest terror attack of all time
A popular Chinese 1-2 in the 200 m butterfly at the Beijing Olympics with Liu Zige swimming a world record of 2:04.18 to beat teammate Jiao Liuyang

Australian women's 4 x 200 m freestyle relay team of Stephanie Rice, Bronte Barratt, Kylie Palmer, and Linda Mackenzie swim a world record 7:44.31 to beat China and take the gold medal at the Beijing Olympics
French swimmer Alain Bernard wins the coveted 100 m freestyle gold medal at the Beijing Olympics, beating Eamon Sullivan of Australia by 0.11 seconds
Japanese swimmer Kosuke Kitajima repeats his Athens Olympics 100/200 m breaststroke double at the Beijing Games, winning the 200 m in an Olympic record of 2:07.64
First Summer Youth Olympic Games open in Singapore
King Duncan I of Scotland is killed in battle against his first cousin and rivalMacbeth, who succeeds him as king (not murdered in his sleep as in Shakespeare's play)
Johann Fustand Peter Schöffer publish the Mainz Psalter, the first printed book with a complete date
Christopher Columbuslands at the mouth of the Orinoco River in what is now Venezuela
Queen Elizabeth Iof England refuses sovereignty of the Netherlands
Cornelis de Houtman's Dutch fleet arrives back in Holland, the first Dutch visit to Java
American naturalistHenry David Thoreaujailed for refusing to pay taxes
Abraham Lincolnreceives the first group of African Americans to confer with a US president
Ceremony to celebrate the completion of Cologne Cathedral attended by EmperorWilhelm I, first begun in 1248, now the largest Gothic cathedral in Northern Europe
Queen Victoriareceives Zulu Chief Cetshwayo
Cecil Rhodes' British colonists reach Masvingo (Fort Victoria), the oldest colonial settlement in Zimbabwe
ChampionJames J. Jeffriesscores a 10th-round TKO win over former championJames J. Corbettin San Francisco in his 6th heavyweight boxing title defense
British Expeditionary Force led by Field MarshalJohn Frenchand General Henry Wilson lands in France
MLB Philadelphia A's Foxx hits for the cycle and sets a new AL record with 9 RBIs in an 11-5 win over the Indians in Cleveland
US PresidentFranklin Rooseveltand British Prime MinisterWinston Churchillissue the joint declaration that later becomes known as the Atlantic Charter
Dwight D. Eisenhoweris named commander for the invasion of North Africa
British Prime MinisterWinston Churchillarrives in Corsica
Ho Chi Minh's Viet Minh movement launch a coordinated uprising against French rule across Vietnam following the Japanese surrender
Babe Didrikson Zahariasgives up amateur status for $300,000
Australian fast bowler Ray Lindwall takes 6-20 as England is dismissed for a record low of 52 in the fifth cricket Test at the Oval;Don Bradmanis out for 0 in his final Test innings

"A Place in the Sun," a film adaptation ofTheodore Dreiser's "An American Tragedy," directed by George Stevens and starringMontgomery Clift,Elizabeth Taylor, andShelley Winters, premieres in Los Angeles, California; it wins six Academy Awards, including Best Director, in 1952
Alan Turing's groundbreaking paper on mathematical biology “The Chemical Basis of Morphogenesis” is published
NASA civilian test pilotJoseph A. Walkertakes the experimental space plane X-15 to 60,000 meters
Sonny & Cher's single "I Got You Babe" hits #1
Celebrity hairdresserVidal Sassooncuts actress Mia Farrow's hair into the famous pixie cut, costing $5,000; in reality, it is just a publicity stunt, as Farrow had previously cut her own hair
Johnny Unitasfiles $725,000 suit against Baltimore Colts
Russian composerDmitri Shostakovichis buried at the Novodevichy Cemetery in Moscow
Democratic Convention in NYC nominatesJimmy Carterfor president andWalter Mondalefor vice president
MLB Philadelphia Phillies'Pete Rose's 12,365th at-bat sets a record, passingHank Aaron
Michael Jacksonbuys ATV Music, including publishing rights to most of the Beatles' song catalog, for $47.5 million; 10 years later, he sells half of his interest to Sony for $150 million
Pakistani opposition leaderBenazir Bhuttois arrested
Oakland'sMark McGwiresets the rookie home run record at 39 en route to 49
PresidentP. W. Bothaof South Africa resigns
MLB Minnesota TwinsDave Winfieldis 23rd to hit 400 home runs
NY Yankees retire Jackson's uniform number 44 onReggie JacksonDay
"Battle of Brit Pop" rival bandsOasis(Roll with It) and Blur (Country House) release singles on the same day
Seattle rock bandFoo Fightersappear on The Late Show withDavid Lettermanin their network television debut, performing their single "This Is A Call"
Convicted Oklahoma City bomberTimothy McVeighformally sentenced to death by Oklahoma Court of Appeals
Winnie Mandelasued by the South African government
DuringKublai Khan's second Mongol invasion of Japan, his invading Chinese fleet of 3,500 vessels disappears in a typhoon near the Japanese coast
Japan's government notifies the Allies that it has accepted the Potsdam Declaration
V-J Day, the Empire of Japan surrenders unconditionally to the Allies, ending World War II (August 15 in Japan and other countries depending on the time zone)
The Beatles tape an appearance for the US variety show "TheEd SullivanShow"
Hanazono, 95th Emperor of Japan (1308-18), Zen priest, and last emperor of the Kamakura period, born in Heian Kyō, Ashikaga shogunate, Japan (d. 1348)
Margaret Pole, 8th Countess of Salisbury, daughter of George, Duke of Clarence, born in Farleigh Hungerford Castle, Somerset, England (d. 1541)
Princess Catherine of York, 9th child and sixth daughter of King Edward IV by his wife Elizabeth Woodville, born in Eltham Palace, Greenwich, England (d. 1527)
Paolo Sarpi, Italian church historian and statesman, born in Venice (d. 1623)
Robert Hayman, English poet, born in Wolborough, Newton Abbot, Devon (d. 1629)
William Hutchinson, Rhode Island colonist, born in Alford, Lincolnshire, England (d. 1642)
Méric Casaubon, French-English classical scholar, born in Geneva (d. 1671)
François de Harlay de Champvallon, Archbishop of Paris, born in Paris (d. 1695)
Daniel Elsevier, Dutch book publisher, born in Leiden, Zuid-Holland, Netherland (d. 1680)
Cosimo III de' Medici, Grand Duke of Tuscany, monarch of Florence (1670-1723), born in Pitti Palace, Florence, Tuscany (d. 1723)
Christopher Monck, 2nd Duke of Albemarle, English statesman who arranged the first boxing match, born in England (d. 1688)
Johann Georg Christian Störl, composer, born in Kirchberg an der Jagst, Duchy of Württemberg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1719)
Frederik Willem I, King of Prussia (bodyguard of giants), born in Berlin, Prussia (d. 1740)
Claude-Joseph Vernet, French painter, born in Avignon, France (d. 1789)
Henriette-Anne of France, twin daughter of king Louis XV, born in Palace of Versailles, France (d. 1752)
Louise Élisabeth of France, twin daughter of king Louis XV, born in Palace of Versailles, France (d. 1759)
Charles Hutton, English mathematician and surveyor, born in Newcastle upon Tyne, England (d. 1823)
Leopold Hofmann, Austrian composer and kapellmeister (Cathedral of St. Stephan, 1772-93), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1793)
Marie Allard, French ballerina (Auguste Vestris), born in Marseille, France (d. 1802)
Carle Vernet, French painter and lithographer, born in Bordeaux, France (d. 1836)
Friedrich Dülon, German flautist and composer, born in Oranienburg, Margraviate of Brandenburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1826)
Francis I, King of the Two Sicilies (1825-30), born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1830)
Francesco Capaccini, Italian cardinal and diplomat, born in Rome (d. 1845)
Letitia Elizabeth Landon, English poet (Heath's Book of Beauty), born in Chelsea, London (d. 1838)
Samuel Sebastian Wesley, British organist and composer, born in London (d. 1876)
Henry Hayes Lockwood, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Kent County, Delaware (d. 1899)
Alexander H. Bailey, American politician (Member of the U.S. House of Representatives from New York), born in Minisink, New York (d. 1874)
Clement H. Stevens, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Norwich, Connecticut (d. 1864)
Karel Miry, Belgian composer (De Vlaamse Leeuw), born in Ghent, Belgium (d. 1889)
William Terry, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Amherst County, Virginia (d. 1888)
Tom King, English boxer (English heavyweight champion 1862-63), rower and bookmaker, born in London, England (d. 1888)
Walter Besant, English writer and philanthropist (Rebel Queen), born in Portsmouth, Hampshire (d. 1901)
Richard von Krafft-Ebing, Austro–German psychiatrist and author (Psychopathia Sexualis), born in Mannheim, Duchy of Baden, German Confederation (d. 1902)
Henry Duff Traill, British author and journalist (The Marquis of Salisbury), born in Blackheath, London (d. 1900)
Robert Comtesse, Swiss politician and member of the Swiss Federal Council (1899-1912), born in Valangin, Sweden (d. 1922)
Earnest [Bachigaloupi] Tourniaire, Dutch actor (Inkwartiering, Kloris & Roses), born in Dordrecht, Zuid-Holland, Nederland (d. 1921)
Max Wagenknecht, German composer, born in Woldisch Tychow, Pomerania, Free State of Prussia (d. 1922)
Ernest T. Seton, naturalist, painter and author (Buffalo Wind-1938), born in South Shields, Durham, England (d. 1946)
Bion J. Arnold, American electrical engineer and inventor, born in Casnovia, Michigan (d. 1942)
Ernest Thayer, American poet who wrote the famous baseball poem "Casey", born in Lawrence, Massachusetts (d. 1940)
Guido Castelnuovo, Italian mathematician, born in Venice (d. 1952)
Charles Jean de la Vallée-Poussin, Belgian mathematician (Prime number theorem), born in Leuven, Belgium (d. 1962)
Cupid Childs, American baseball player, born in Calvert County, Maryland (d. 1912)
John Galsworthy, English author (Forsyte Saga-Nobel 1932), born in Kingston upon Thames, Surrey, England (d. 1933)
Leone Sinigaglia, Italian composer, born in Turin, Italy (d. 1944)
Armas Järnefelt, Finnish conductor and composer (Berceuse), born in Vyborg, Russian Empire (d. 1958)
Guangxu, Chinese Emperor of the Qing dynasty (1875-1908), born in Prince Chun's Mansion, Beijing, China (d. 1908)
Mustafa Kamil Pasha, Egyptian lawyer, journalist and nationalist activist, born in Cairo, Egypt (d. 1908)
Alexander I [Obrenović], King of Serbia (1889-1903), born in Belgrade, Serbia (d. 1903)
Sibilla Aleramo [Rina Faccio], Italian feminist and poet (Una Donna), born in Alessandria, Italy (d. 1960)
Harald Kidde, Danish writer (Aage og Else), born in Vejle, Denmark (d. 1918)
Fred Alexander, American tennis player (Australasian C'ship 1908, 5-time US Indoor winner), born in Sea Bright, New Jersey (d. 1969)
Gisela Richter, English art historian, born in London (d. 1972)

Ernest Everett Just, African-American embryologist known for his pioneering work in the physiology of development, especially fertilization and cell division, born in Charleston, South Carolina (d. 1941)
Marija Leiko, Latvian film actress (Falscher Start), born in Riga, Russian Empire (d. 1937)
Robert Woolsey, American vaudevillian (Wheeler & Woolsey), and comic actor (The Nitwits; Hold 'em Jail; High Flyers; Rainmakers), born in Carbondale, Illinois (d. 1938)
Marinus de Jong, Dutch composer, born in Oosterhout, Netherlands (d. 1984)
Kaikhosru Shapurji Sorabji [Leon Dudley Sorabji], Parsi-British pianist and composer (Opus clavicembalisticum), born in Chingford, Essex, England (d. 1988)
Tommy Scott, West Indian cricket spin bowler (8 Tests, 22 wickets; Jamaica), born in Kingston, Jamaica (d. 1961)
Alfred Alessandrescu, Romanian composer, born in Bucharest, Kingdom of Hungary, Austria-Hungary (d. 1959)
Carl Benton Reid, American actor (Pressure Point, In a Lonely Place), born in Lansing, Michigan (d. 1973)

Frank Burge, Australian rugby league forward (13 Tests, 26 games NSW; Glebe, St. George; RL "Immortal") and coach (St. George, North Sydney, Western Suburbs, Newtown), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1958)
Jack Gregory, Australian cricket all-rounder (24 Tests, 2 x 100, 7 x 50, 85 wickets, BB 7/69; NSW CA), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1973)
Jaroslav Jeremiáš, Czech pianist and composer, born in Pisek, Kingdom of Bohemia Austria-Hungary (d. 1919)
James Pitman, British educator and spelling reformer (Alphabets and Reading: The Initial Teaching Alphabet), born in London (d. 1985)
John Ringling North, American circus director (Ringling Bros. and Barnum & Bailey Circus from 1937-43), born in Baraboo, Wisconsin (d. 1985)
Millard Mitchell, Cuban actor (12 O'Clock High, Gunfighter), born in Havana, Cuba (d. 1953)
Nicolás Cotoner, 23rd Marquess of Mondéjar, Spanish nobleman and military officer, born in Palma de Mallorca, Spain (d. 1996)
Stanley Adams, American lyricist and songwriter ("What a Diff'rence a Day Makes"), born in Manhattan, New York City (d. 1994)
Jean Knox (née Jeanette Marcia Marshall), British Baroness, and military administrator (Director of the Auxiliary Territorial Service, 1941-43), born in the UK (d. 1993)
Manos Katrakis, Greek actor (Antigone; Marinos Kontaras), born in Kastelli Kissamou, Crete (d. 1984)
(Hezekiah) "Stuff" Smith, American jazz violinist, vocalist, and composer ("It's Wonderful"; "You'se A Viper"), born in Portsmouth, Ohio (d. 1967)[1]
Len Darling, Australian cricketer (South Australian & Test batsman of 30's), born in South Yarra, Victoria, Australia (d. 1992)
Pierre Schaeffer, French electronics engineer and composer, born in Nancy, France (d. 1995)
Willy Ronis, French photographer (Paris in Color), born in Paris (d. 2009)
Ethel Lois Payne, American civil rights journalist and "1st Lady of the Black Press", born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1991)
Jan Koetsier, Dutch composer, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 2006)
Pieter Platteel, Dutch civil servant and last governor Dutch New Guinea, born in Utrecht, Netherlands (d. 1978)
Eva Strittmatter, German writer (Tinko), born in Spremberg, Niederlausitz, Germany (d. 1930)
Karel Poons, Jewish-Dutch ballet dancer, educator, and Holocaust survivor, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1994)
Fred Davis, English snooker player (World C'ship 1948-49, 51), born in Chesterfield, Derbyshire (d. 1998)
Paul Dean, American baseball player (St. Louis Cardinals), born in Lucas, Arkansas (d. 1981)
B.A. Santamaria, Australian political activist and journalist, born in Melbourne (d. 1998)
Victor Mishcon, Baron Mishcon, British solicitor and a Labour politician, born in London (d. 2006)
Wellington Mara, Co-Owner of the New York Giants, born in Rochester, New York (d. 2005)
Giorgio Strehler, Italian opera and theatre director, born in Barcola, Trieste, Italy (d. 1997)
Alice Ghostley, American singer and actress (Bewitched, Designing Women), born in Eve, Vernon County, Missouri (d. 2007)
Jorja Curtright, American stage and screen actress (Whistle Stop, Heaven Only Knows), and interior decorator, born in Amarillo, Texas (d. 1985)
William Flanagan, American composer, and music critic, born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 1969)
Georges Prêtre, French classical and opera conductor known for interpretation of Poulenc, born in Waziers, France (d. 2017)
Paul Beaver, American experimental musician, electronic, and psychedelic rock Moog synthesizer player (Beaver & Krause), born in Columbiana, Ohio (d. 1975)
Russell Baker, American columnist, author and humorist (NY Times), born in Loudoun County, Virginia (d. 2019)

(Armando) "Buddy" Greco, American jazz and pop singer ("Ooh! Look-A There, Ain't She Pretty"; "The Lady Is A Tramp"), pianist, and television personality (Away We Go), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2017)
Alice Adams, American author (Beautiful Girl), born in Fredericksburg, Virginia (d. 1999)
Frank Cvitanovich, Canadian filmmaker, born in Vancouver (d. 1995)
Lord Whaddon, CEO (Daltrade & Skorimpex-Rind), born in Sale, Cheshire (d. 2005)
Laurens van Deenen, Dutch biochemist, born in Maastricht, Germany (d. 1994)
Lina Wertmüller [Arcangela Braueich], Italian screenwriter, film director, and 1st woman nominated for an Academy Award for Directing (Seven Beauties), born in Rome, Italy (d. 2021)
Dick Tiger, Nigerian professional boxer, born in Amaigbo, Nigeria (d. 1971)
Lorez Alexandria [Taylor] American jazz and gospel singer (Alexandria the Great), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2001)
Louise Slaughter, American politician (Member of the U.S. House of Representatives from New York), born in Lynch, Kentucky (d. 2018)
Oliver Neville, English educator (principal of RADA Royal Academy of Dramatic Arts), born in Birmingham, England (d. 2021)
Thomas Meehan, American writer (Annie, The Producers, Hairspray), born in Ossining, New York (d. 2017)
Gerald Solomon, American businessman and politician (Member of the U.S. House of Representatives from New York), born in Okeechobee, Florida (d. 2001)
Roger Chorley, 2nd Baron Chorley, English accountant and peer (Chorley Report, President of the Royal Geographical Society), born in England (d. 2016)
Frederic Raphael, American-British writer (After the War, California Time), born in Chicago, Illinois
Barry Frank, American television sports impresario (CBS, ABC and IMG), born in Columbus, Ohio (d. 2019)
James V. Hansen, American politician (Member of the U.S. House of Representatives from Utah), born in Salt Lake City, Utah (d. 2018)
Lee Hoffman, American author (The Valdez Horses), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2007)
Bryce Courtenay, Australian novelist (The Power of One), born in Johannesburg, South Africa (d. 2012)
Richard R. Ernst, Swiss physical chemist (Nobel Prize - 1991, techniques for high-resolution nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy), born in Winterthur, Switzerland (d. 2021)
André Boniface, French rugby union outside back (48 Tests; Stade Montois Rugby; IRB HOF), born in Montfort-en-Chalosse, France (d. 2024)
Trevor Bannister, British actor (Are You Being Served?), born in Durrington, Wiltshire (d. 2011)
Brian Curvis, Welsh boxer and British welterweight champion (1960-1964), born in Swansea, Wales (d. 2012)
Brian Fennelly, American classical composer (A Thoreau Symphony), born in Kingston, New York (d. 2015)
